candidates.why_suggested: Best local example of a scientific trace figure where line panels are the primary data object rather than a lightweight summary overlay.
design_takeaways: Trace figures need enough whitespace and axis discipline so waveform shape remains readable.
Event annotations should explain the line, not compete with it.
Multi-panel trace figures work when each panel has one role: raw trace, zoomed event, derived frequency curve, or exemplar response.
what_to_borrow: Line figure broken into functional subpanels

candidates.why_suggested: Best reusable style-system reference for multi-line styling, line style differentiation, and publication-minded rcParams defaults.
design_takeaways: Line styling can separate series with dash patterns and markers when color alone is insufficient.
Publication-style defaults for tick direction, spine control, and serif/sans switching matter for line-heavy figures.
A style library helps the agent separate line grammar from project-specific data.

Best local example of a manuscript-quality embedding scatter where marker shape, color, and sparse annotation work together without visual overload.
design_takeaways: Use point cloud structure as the primary message and let annotation only stabilize reading.
Marker shape can encode one categorical layer when color is already semantically occupied.
Cluster envelopes or sparse group labels can be cleaner than a dense legend.

candidates.why_suggested: Useful style-system reference for agent implementation because it demonstrates reusable scatter construction patterns instead of one-off paper code.
design_takeaways: Scatter templates should expose marker size, alpha, and color decisions as parameters.
Correlation or regression variants should be explicit choices, not hidden defaults.
A reusable scatter API helps the agent separate chart type from project-specific data.
what_to_borrow: Controlled progression from plain scatter to correlation or regression variants

candidates.why_suggested: Strongest local atlas-style embedding reference for clustered manifold maps with restrained point styling, on-figure labels, and local structure preserved well enough for manuscript reading.
design_takeaways: Atlas embeddings should usually privilege on-data labels over giant legends.
Cluster ellipses or outlines can work if they clarify group structure without hiding the points.
When the embedding itself is abstract, axis clutter can be removed entirely.
what_to_borrow: Point-cloud restraint with readable group structure
Embedding panels with minimal axis furniture

candidates.why_suggested: Best local reference when the embedding needs a grey density background with only a few targets or modalities highlighted in color and marker shape.
design_takeaways: Density-highlight embeddings work best when the non-target population is pushed into a quiet grey layer.
A small number of highlighted targets can carry both color and marker semantics without collapsing readability.
Highlight panels should show enough background structure that the selected points still have spatial context.
